claim: Janet retrieves knowledge by computing argmax dot(query_vector, entry_vector) over the corpus — the Born rule: the entry with highest amplitude alignment wins. This is softmax at T=0, the zero-temperature limit of transformer attention. Add entries with know.py or the MCP add tool.

Janet's corpus lives in .janet/knowledge/*.jsonl — one JSON object per line, each with id, domain, claim, tags, confidence. Janet finds .janet/ by walking up the directory tree, like git finds .git/. Each project gets its own corpus. Query with: query('your question here').

Janet encodes text in two spaces: wave (co-occurrence centroid — meaning, evolves with corpus) and particle (FNV hash — identity, fixed). Queries use wave encoding for semantic retrieval. The tool center uses particle encoding for exact keyword routing. Same Born-rule primitive, two knowledge centers.

The correct cycle: (1) query Janet before acting — one MCP call, one second, prevents contradicting verified structure. (2) act with that context. (3) verify with operator. (4) add verified conclusions to corpus. Quality over quantity: one precise atomic entry beats ten vague ones.

Landauer's principle: erasing one bit of information costs at least kT·ln2 joules of energy. Copying is free (reversible). Erasure is irreversible. This connects information theory to thermodynamics — every irreversible computation has a minimum energy cost. Verified experimentally by Bérut et al. 2012.

Born rule: the probability of measuring outcome x given quantum state ψ is P(x) = |⟨x|ψ⟩|² — the squared amplitude of the projection. Measurement is irreversible collapse. The Born rule is the bridge between quantum amplitudes and classical probabilities. It applies at every scale where measurement occurs.

P vs NP: does every problem whose solution can be verified in polynomial time also have a polynomial-time algorithm? P ⊆ NP trivially. Whether NP ⊆ P is unknown — the central open problem in computer science. Most researchers believe P ≠ NP. A proof either way wins the Clay Millennium Prize ($1M).

Folk theorem: in infinitely repeated games, any outcome that gives each player at least their minmax payoff can be sustained as a Nash equilibrium through trigger strategies. Cooperation is achievable among self-interested agents when the game repeats indefinitely. The shadow of the future enforces cooperation.
